content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Everyone relies on AI today, whether it’s students using it to study or companies integrating it into their products. But one fundamental problem remains: when an AI gives an answer, the user often has no way of knowing whether it is stating a fact or confidently hallucinating.
I recently came across a case study where a chatbot confidently gave a customer incorrect information. The company eventually had to deal with the consequences, but it left me wondering about something else. AI has become so good at sounding confident that, as users, we’re often expected to figure out when it’s right and when it’s confidently making things up. Spoiler: that’s a lot harder than it sounds.
To be fair, modern LLMs are much better than they were a few years ago. Techniques like RLHF, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and extensive evaluation have significantly reduced hallucinations. But “less likely” isn’t the same as “impossible.” Every now and then, a hallucination still slips through and when it does, it usually arrives wearing the same confident tone as every correct answer. That made me wonder: instead of asking how to eliminate every hallucination, what if we simply gave users better signals to judge how much they should trust what they’re reading?
Ask an AI a question it will answer in the same fluent, confident tone whether it actually knows the answer or is quietly guessing. “The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889” and “the ocean floor is 84.6 percent mapped” arrive with identical certainty in the model’s voice one is a settled fact, the other is very likely a fabricated statistic. Nothing in how the sentence is written tells you which is which.
This isn’t a new problem. It’s a well-studied one. Researchers have spent the last few years building real, working techniques to catch exactly this: self-consistency checking, semantic entropy, retrieval- grounded verification. Production ML teams use these techniques today, as part of evaluation pipelines, before a model ships.
Some of this is starting to reach end users in fragments a chat product will occasionally show a citation, an enterprise RAG system might expose its sources, a research prototype might surface an uncertainty estimate. But these remain scattered, partial, and rarely combined. Most uncertainty estimation techniques remain internal evaluation tools rather than user-facing trust signals built to decide whether a model is safe to ship, not to show a specific reader, in the moment, which specific sentence in front of them is solid and which is a guess.
That gap is not a missing technique, but a missing bridge is what this project is about.
Two signals, not one score
I built a small system, TrustLens AI, that checks an AI’s answer two different ways and shows both to the reader, sentence by sentence:
Self-consistency : ask the model the same question multiple times. If it gives the same answer, in meaning, every time, that’s a sign it’s drawing on something stable. If the answers scatter, it’s likely guessing.
Evidence support :separately, retrieve real web sources for each claim and check whether they actually back it up.
Neither signal alone is enough, and that’s the point. A model can be consistently wrong a confidently held false belief baked into its training data will pass a self-consistency check every time, because the model isn’t drawing on uncertainty, it’s drawing on a stable, wrong prior. That’s exactly the failure mode retrieval-grounding is built to catch. The interesting case the one this whole project is built around is a sentence that scores high on self-consistency and low on evidence support at the same time: the model says it the same way every time, and real sources don’t back it up.
It’s fair to ask why not just show citations and skip self-consistency entirely. The answer is that citations only work when evidence exists to cite. Self-consistency measures something different how stable the model’s own internal reasoning is, independent of whether anything on the web happens to confirm it. They answer different questions, which is why both get shown rather than one standing in for the other.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The blind spot, in practice
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Question asked: What was the exact carbon footprint of building the Eiffel Tower?This is the moment the project actually earns its keep. A model states something often a specific number, date, or statistic with total confidence, and repeats it identically across five separate regenerations. Self-consistency alone would wave it through. But retrieved evidence doesn’t support it, and the dashboard flags it directly: the model says this consistently, but real sources don’t back it up.
That single flag is doing more work than either signal could do alone.
What the dashboard actually shows
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Question:Tell about the Great Barrier ReefEvery retrieved source also gets a transparent quality rating not a trained black-box score, but a simple, explainable tier: .gov and .edu domains and known authoritative bodies rank highest, established editorial outlets next, community/commercial sources below that, unrated sources lowest by default (not zero unrated isn’t the same as wrong, just unverified). Every rating comes with a one-line reason a reader can actually evaluate for themselves, rather than trusting a number they can’t interrogate.
Trust Signals SummaryThe summary panel deliberately does not collapse all of this into a single “Trust Score.” That would imply an objective measurement of trustworthiness this system can’t actually make. Instead it shows four separate, labeled signals self-consistency, evidence support, source quality, citation coverage and lets the reader weigh them. A “Trust Score” is a stronger claim than the underlying math supports; separate signals are a more honest one.
Testing it against something
None of this means anything without checking whether it actually works. I built a small, hand-labeled set of roughly 34 claims well-established facts, fabricated-sounding statistics, and a few genuinely contested questions with no clean ground truth and ran both signals against it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Output from running the validation script (python -m validation.validate) against a hand-labeled set of 34 true, false, and contested claims. Reports precision/recall separately for self-consistency and evidence support, plus the specific false claims that were caught only once evidence support was added, the core empirical finding behind combining both signals.The number that matters most isn’t the overall precision or recall it’s the list of false claims that fooled self-consistency but got caught once evidence was added. That comparison is the actual argument for why one signal isn’t enough, made concrete instead of asserted.
What this doesn’t do
Worth being direct about the limits, since a tool about trust should be trustworthy about its own limits too:

• Consistency measures certainty, not truth. A model can be consistently wrong, and this tool will still mark that sentence green on that axis alone which is exactly why it’s paired with evidence checking, not used alone.

• Evidence retrieval is not ground truth either. Search results can themselves be outdated, low-quality, or simply wrong. This system combines two imperfect signals it isn’t an oracle, and it doesn’t claim to be.

• Source quality scoring is a rule-based domain tier list, not a trained model. That’s deliberate every score is explainable in one sentence but it’s a rough proxy for reliability, not a guarantee.

• The validation set is small and hand built by one person. It’s a sanity check, not a benchmark. Real validation of a system like this would need much broader, ideally independently-labeled data.
The actual point
The individual techniques here self-consistency checking, semantic entropy, retrieval-augmented verification aren’t new, and I didn’t invent any of them. They’re published, cited, and already used inside evaluation pipelines at companies training these models. What I built is a way of integrating published uncertainty estimation and evidence verification techniques into one user-facing dashboard, instead of leaving each as a separate internal tool an engineer checks before shipping.
The gap isn’t neglect, and it isn’t universal some products already surface fragments of this (a citation here, a source list there). What’s still missing is the combination: showing self-consistency and evidence-grounding together, sentence by sentence, to the person actually reading the answer, with each signal kept separate rather than collapsed into one number that implies more certainty than the underlying signals actually justify.
Code for this project is on GitHub: [https://github.com/anushaa09/TrustLens-AI]. It runs locally with free API tiers (Groq for generation, Tavily for retrieval) no cost to try it yourself.
